sentences.text: I have to go one step back.
Before we were liberated and the Germans still were there, they made us clean up a tent.
This huge tent was filled with bodies, so they made us drag the bodies through these mass graves.
At night, when they said stop, you will see a row-- a road of bodies.
They thought if they don't bury the body that the typhus would spread faster, and that they would-- it would-- you know, it would kill us all.
It nearly happened, because most of the people who were there left were very ill.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very fortunate that I-- I don't know how or why-- I didn't get sick.
So as I said, the soldiers came and they tried to help us.
They set up these showers.
They brought food in, which was very bad.
They brought the K rations, which was poison for us.
After all, we didn't eat for a long time.
And the beans, Heinz beans, very hard to digest.
Spam, and all these things.
I didn't eat it.
I drank milk, powdered milk.
And that really saved me.
Because I don't know where I got this idea that I shouldn't eat all of that and just drink, and I-- that's what I did.
So I didn't get sick.
A lot of people died after eating all that food because our stomachs just could not take it.
So after they finished and they took all the sick people out, then they took the few of us who we were capable of walking.
I was very skinny.
After all, I'm a tall girl.
And they brought us to Bergen.
That's where all the sick people were also.
They-- Bergen was a town of, also, armories.
And this-- these were made into hospital buildings.
They brought the-- the British brought nurses and doctors to look after all the sick people.
I went to work.
I went to work in the kitchen.
I always thought that being near food is a good idea.
So I was in the kitchen and we got from-- I think from Denmark barrels of butter and we got half a cow.
I don't know whether we had a cook or something.
I can't recall that.
I only know that I worked in the kitchen.
And the girls who were with me on-- living there, they had it very good because I would bring huge pieces of butter.
And what did I do with the butter?
I put it in here.
And by the time I came to my room, it kind of moved down.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had dances.
Soldiers came.
I remember there were flyers.
And one of them, he said, I'm from Canada.
And he spoke French.
I couldn't figure it out.
He's from Canada, and he speaks French?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The soldiers were allowed to-- to be with us.
They were allowed to take us to movies.
My-- my first movie I saw was with Rita Hayworth, which I thought was wonderful.
And we all smoked in that movie, and you hardly could see the screen for all the smoke.
I think this young man's name, if I can recall, was Goldsmith.
And he was from-- Liverpool?
Edinburgh?
Anyway, he used to come for me and we would go for walks.
And then they said that we were allowed to write.
I remembered my uncle's address in Canada.
After all, we had to keep all the things we wanted to remember in our minds, because we had no paper, no pencil.
We couldn't keep it anywhere because they took everything away.
But I remembered 1448 Bishop Street in Montreal.
So I wrote a letter to my uncle.
And I believe it was one of the first letters ever to arrive in Canada from someone who survived the war.
And my uncle told me that it was published in I don't have a copy of it.
So I told him that I'm alive.
I'm the only one from the family.
And-- and I stayed on a while.
I did not want to stay in Germany.
And I had a friend whose husband found out that she was alive.
I knew them from 19-- from 1940.
We were in the Zionist organization together, and I believe also in school.
And he arrived with a truck.
So I said, are you going to take me along?
Oh, sure.
So it was the [?
Rodens. ?] And they also did a tape-- Dr.
Roden ?] and Eva.
And then there was a girl-- a friend of mine by the name-- I don't-- her name was [?
Ornstein, ?] but they used to call her [?
Chenda. ?] And then there was Ruth Bondy, who is today a journalist in Israel.
And she wrote a very wonderful book about Terezin, and it's called And so we took off.
And we drove through Germany, and we came to the Czech border, where we stood up and we sang the national anthem.
And we stayed overnight.
I think we got good and drunk, because, after all, we were free now, and why not celebrate?
And then the next day we drove into Prague.
They left off in Prague.
And [?
Chenda ?] and I, we really didn't have where to go.
I knew that I have no one there.
And she didn't have anyone either.
So there came a woman along.
And she said, you know what?
I-- my husband and I returned, and we got our flat back.
If you want, you can stay with us.
Wonderful.
She gave us the address.
It was-- [INAUDIBLE] the street was called [INAUDIBLE].
It's in the old town.
And so we took the street car.
But we had no money.
So a soldier paid for us, and we came to [INAUDIBLE] and to the apartment.
So there was this lady, and her husband, and her cousin, and another cousin.
There was a whole bunch of us.
And we lived together for a while.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know how it is?
You hear by mouth all the information where you should go, what you should do, and so--
You have to understand that there was no one who said, OK, you came back.
You have no one.
We are going to send you to a hospital.
You get some psychiatric help.
Like, you know, when the Americans were in Iran and they brought them back, they  were hostages.
Right away, they took them to the hospital and they-- they did all kinds of things.
Nobody cared about us.
Nobody.
We just had to fend for ourselves.
So you-- you went where the survivors or the-- at that time we didn't call ourselves survivors.
The people who came back from camp congregated.
And there was a hall.
And people were looking-- you know, the pictures.
Do you know this one?
Do you know-- did you meet this one?
[INAUDIBLE] I am the brother of this one, and so on.
And what did I find there?
I found the picture of Hanus Gerst.
They were looking for him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I remembered also the address where the Mrs.
Gerstl lived.
But before I went there, I met other people and we went to nunneries for lunch.
You know, we were standing in line and I had a-- I think I had a leather jacket, which I found in Bergen-Belsen in some warehouse or something.
And there were some-- there were two young men standing beside me and they said, listen, would you like to barter your jacket for a winter-- for a coat?
I said, sure.
So I took my-- OK, we meet here at the nunnery again tomorrow.
And you bring your jacket and I'll bring your coat.
I said, fine.
And we would exchange views where the food was better.
Was it this nunnery or that nunnery or this Cloister or the [?
Harita's or wherever? ?] And so we went from one-- they fed us, because we didn't have anything.
We didn't-- and then we got-- I think 500 crowns we got from the Jewish community, from the social agency.
And I went to look for Mrs.
Gerstl.
I wanted to know what happened with Hanus.
So I went where she used to live, and I couldn't find her name there.
So I went to the superintendent.
I said, do you-- I'm looking for the Gerstls.
Do you know where they are?
Oh, yes, they live now in [?
Karlin. ?] They moved from here-- that part of Prague.
And so she gave me the address, and I went looking for them.
Then I told them who I was.
And of course they were very sad that-- Mr.
Gerstl went, in 1940, to Palestine.
And then he joined the Czech Army, and he went through North Africa-- the Battle of El Alamein.
And from there, he went to England.
And then, again, he was with the Czech Army and went through Europe.
He was a quartermaster.
That means that he was looking after all the food, which was kind of a good thing.
And when he was discharged, he could buy the food.
So when-- when I came to the Gerstls, they had everything there.
So they said, look, you're alone.
You can come-- you can come and eat with us and that's fine.
So I-- I came and ate.
I came there once or three times a week.
I don't remember.
And then they said, look.
You have no one.
You might as well stay with us.
So I moved in with them.
And Mr.
Gerstl was a very practical man, and he said to me, now, what are your plans?
What do you want to do?
I said, well, I do not want to stay in Europe.
Europe wasn't very kind to me.
I have   no one here.
I want to leave.
I have an uncle in Canada, and that's where I want to go.
So he says, well, first things first.
If you want to go to Canada, you had better start learning-- studying English.
So I-- I went to   a language school.
I went a whole year.
I passed my university exam.
And I still didn't get a visa.
I was in touch with my uncle, but I didn't get a visa to Canada.
So Mr.
Gerstl had a friend who opened a store.
So I was the-- I worked in the store.
There was nothing to sell.
I had three pairs of gloves, I think, and-- nothing.
What was Prague like?
Prague was a funny city after the war, because you had people who never lived in Prague.
You had people from all over Europe who came back and-- and they came through Prague, and they liked it, and they stayed there.
And it started to become normal.
It was very poor.
People didn't have anything.
You know, the war lasted a long time.
Prague was not bombed.
It was-- I think they had three air raids, and it was in the middle of the city.
That's all what happened.
It was not demolished otherwise.
So I worked in the store for a little bit, and-- and one morning, I came and it was broken in.
I don't know why they broke in, because there was nothing what to steal.
So my job was finished.
And I got a job with the Jewish community where I was sorting clothes.
We got some clothes back from Terezin, which we were sorting.
And then there was a division of clothes coming from America.
People donated, and we were sorting that.

I was working there for a few months, and my uncle-- who was not really my uncle, he was a first cousin of my mother-- he became-- he was brought back from Israel or-- yes, Israel at that time-- as chief rabbi of Prague.
His name was Dr.
Gustav Zicher.
And so they thought a relative of Dr.
Zicher couldn't work there in this horrible place looking after all those schmattas.
So they-- they brought me to the Jewish community, and I worked for a newspaper called [INAUDIBLE], which today is called differently.
I think or something.
And I was working there.
I was transcribing articles.
Like, people who submitted articles, hand written, I would type it out, or-- and then we had a place for people who had [INAUDIBLE] who subscribed to it, and things like that.
I worked there.
And one day, I went downstairs to the-- to the mail room.
And there was a man who knew me, and he said, oh, you speak English?
I said, yes.
He said, you know I have a good job for you.
I said, what is it?
Oh, they-- they're looking for somebody at the American Joint Distribution Committee-- distribution committee.
And I said, gee, that would be good.
So I got this job, and it paid much better than the one I had.
And that's where I-- that's where I went.
And I-- since I had a job, the Gerstls said, no, that now I'm on my own, I should live by myself.
So they found a room for me to stay.
It was not very good.
I found another room.
And so I was rooming around there until I found a kind of nice room with a family, [?
Stumpf, ?] in a villa.
It was a small room, but it was nice.
There son-- the lady-- Mrs.
Gerstl didn't know, but Mr.
Gerstl knew that he died on the 15th of January in Dachau.
And so, since I was too old for them to adopt me, because there was not-- I was already grown up-- they adopted a small boy by the name of Peter.
So we consider each other-- like, we consider ourselves as brother and sister, since we were kind of adopted by them.
He lives, today, in Austria.
He has three children.
He came to see me two years ago.
And we keep in constant contact.
And so I finally got-- I worked at the American Joint.
And I tried to be a teenager again.
I went a lot to movies.
I liked the theater.
I went dancing every Saturday and Sunday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- you know, one-- one didn't talk about camp.
It-- that was the strangest thing.
You just didn't talk about that.
You tried to kind of start where you left off.
And then, finally, after four years-- oh, yes, I had  a distant cousin also working at the Joint, as we called it.
And she was saying, you know what?
In Australia, they have a shortage of young girls.
And they asked us to register girls who want to go to Australia.
Why don't you register?
So I did, and I got a visa to Canada one day and a visa to Australia the next day.
But I said, Australia is too far.
I'm going to Canada.
How long did you wait for this visa?
Four years.
Yes, I went back.
I went back to-- to Listany, and it was just before all the people who-- who lived there were moved out.
And as I was there, I asked the mayor that I would like to know who took all the things.
So he made a list.
People had to write down what they took.
One had a horse, and the other one had a wash stand, and the third one had a washing machine-- not a washing-- a sewing machine.
And another had a cupboard.
So they wrote it all down.
But at that time-- oh, yes.
And he wanted that I should open the store again.
He says, Miss Epstein, you know we haven't got a store.
Why don't you open your store?
I says, no way.
I'm not going to sit in this village.
I'm living in the city.
I wouldn't come back.
What would I do?
A young girl stay there.
But there was a woman-- her daughter, Mitzi, was my best friend.
And she saw me, and she started screaming and yelling, and she said, you are alive, and my Mitzi is dead.
She was killed by a bomb.
She worked in a town by the name of [?
Toska, ?] and there was an air raid.
She was a maid at a dentist, and she was killed.
And here, I was walking, and her Mitzi was dead.
Well, I went there-- I was there twice in the village, and then I didn't go back, because I knew I wouldn't no anybody, because they all were moved out.
Because all the German people-- speaking people, they moved-- the Czechs moved them to Germany.
Well, you know, they said they want to-- when Hitler was in power, they said they wanted to go home in the Reich.
So the Czechs said, you want to go home in the Reich?
We will help you.
So they moved them all out.
I find it, looking back at it, not fair because the people in Listany-- or Lichtenstein, as it was then known-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they weren't Nazis.
First of all, they-- they didn't even know what it was.
They didn't have a newspaper-- they didn't have a newspaper.
They didn't have a radio.
They were so busy with their fields.
They were so busy with their cows.
Who cared what was going on?
Nobody did anything to them.
So they-- they weren't-- they weren't antisemites.
They weren't against us.
When we started-- when they saw that my mother and we were leaving, they said, Elsa-- they called her car by her first name.
What are you doing?
Where are you going?
She said, I have small children.
I'm afraid of them, and that's why I'm leaving.
So when I came back, and they moved those people out, I knew I wouldn't know anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I left for Canada.
In June 1949, I arrived at my uncle.
My uncle had a beauty parlor.
He told me that I should learn to be a beautician.
And I said, yes.
And I always told him that I would be a beautician.
And of course, I knew I wouldn't be a beautician.
So I came, and I was doing manicures.
So he said, you know how to give a manicure?
I said, yes.
So he said, you know, I'm sending you to [INAUDIBLE].
And let them-- they should do everything they can with your nails so you will know.
And so I-- I went to-- to [INAUDIBLE].
They did everything to my nails, and the next day I was  a manicurist.
And it-- it didn't last long.
I was there two weeks.
And I couldn't take what the people were talking about-- their birds and their dogs and their-- anyway.
Oskar Epstein.
Yes, I lived with him.
Yes, he had two daughters.
His wife was Ann, or Antoinette, and the daughter was Caroline and Ruth.
Caroline died not-- a few years ago.
And Ruth lives in [INAUDIBLE], in St.
Catherine, and she has three daughters.
My English was very good.
I could make myself-- I mean, I had-- I have an accent now.
I probably had an accent much worse then.
But I didn't have any problems with English.
At that time, people didn't ask many questions.
They said, you see, this is my niece, and she spent the war years in Europe.
She lost her family.
She's the only one.
And that's how far it went.
Nobody asked really very much.
I think, in later years, you know, people became more aware of it.
I went to work for a company by the name of [?
Reach ?] Brothers.
My uncle knew them, and Mrs.
Reach ?] came.
She was a-- a customer.
And when she came, he asked whether they didn't have a job for me, because I didn't-- he knew that I didn't want to stay in the beauty parlor.
I wanted to work in a bank, because I-- the little money I have-- I had, I put it in the bank of commerce.
And I said to my uncle that I would like to work in the bank.
And he said that I must be crazy, because they don't take Jewish girls to work in the bank.
And I said, what about Eatons?
Oh, he says, they don't take any Jews to work.
So he-- he asked Mrs.
Reach ?] whether perhaps her husband would-- could find a job for me.
And yes, he did, and I-- I got a job.
I was doing invoicing.
And there was this man who said he would come and fetch me in the morning and take me.
And he fetched me in the morning and he fetched me.
And he became my husband.
After one year, I got married, and--
In Ottawa, because my brother-in-law was the reverend, so he married us.
And we have two children-- Elsa, who is an adopted child, and Victor, who is a natural child.
Elsa lives in Montreal.
She has two daughters.
And her older daughter is married and she has a child.
That makes me a great grandmother.
And my son has three children-- three girls.
And he lives in Orinda, which is in the East Bay, near San Francisco.
My husband's name is Sidney.
Unfortunately, my husband passed away on the 1st of May, 1996, which is not too long ago.
He was very ill.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a very hard time for me.
He was a very jolly person.
He was a very-- he loved his job as a sales manager, because he loved people.
He collected anything which could be collected.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He liked to play the piano, entertain people.
He had a good singing voice.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was much older than me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He didn't always like what I was doing, because I went back to school.
I went to university.
I went to Loyola where I graduated in 1974-- the last year of Loyola.
I studied fine arts and literature.
And so when-- at the beginning, when I only could go in the evening, when I left after supper, I would make dinner and I would wash dishes.
And I would say, now I'm going.
And he would say, so where are you going?
He knew very well that I was going to school, but he just couldn't fathom [LAUGHS] that I was going to school.
So I was married 46 years, and now, I'm alone.
But I'm very busy.
I'm back at school.
I'm taking three-- three computer courses, which are quite difficult.
And I will travel.
I went back to Czechoslovakia-- or now it's called Czech Republic.
I went back after 46 and 1/2 years.
People were quite surprised that I'm speaking still Czech very fluently, even though it's not my mother tongue.
I met people whom I knew through-- after-- when I came back-- two young-- at that time, we were young girls, with whom I went dancing.
One is a widow also.
One never got married.
I-- I met a man whom I knew before I left.
He was-- he's a surgeon.
It was very nice.
He took me to the opera.
He took me to dinner.
We had-- we had very nice times together.
It was-- it was really funny.
It seemed like the time in between does not exist.
I'm back, like, 46 years.
No.
[LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So now I'm back at home, and-- and I'm trying to make the best of it.
And I hope that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was kept alive so that I could be a witness to what happened, because we have so many people who are deniers of the Holocaust.
And I think that I was left alive to be the witness and to be the spokesperson for six million who do not have a voice anymore.
Oh, yes.
Yes, that was my purpose of my trip to go to Czechoslovakia to see Mrs.
Gerstl, who is today 95 years old.
And it-- she came to be with me 20 years ago.
But my son said, Ma, you better go and see her.
You know, she's 95.
She's not going to wait for you much longer.
So you better go.
So I-- it was on his urging me that I went, and I'm really very happy that I did.
I stayed with her.
She cooked every day for me.
She looked after me like I would be little girl.
And I arranged for her, at the Jewish Community, that they would bring her meals so she won't have to cook that much and it will be easier for you-- for her.
And I had to promise her that I'm going to see her again, so I do hope that I will go in spring again to see her.
She is a great lady.
Yes, very much so.
I was always very concerned where they were and-- and when my son-- my son was a competitive swimmer.
He-- he traveled a great deal.
And when he-- let's say he went out of town somewhere, he knew that he had to phone me.
He said, you know, ma, nobody has to phone their parents except me.
I said, yes, I know.
But I want to know how you are.
I was very protective of-- of them.
I also was very strict.
